params.text:
Dignity is in the small things

Dignity is upheld or eroded in ordinary, everyday moments — often more through how you do something than what you do.

  • How you speak to people: use the name they prefer, speak to them rather than over them, and never use childish or belittling language like "good girl" to an adult.
  • How you speak about people: never discuss someone's care or body in front of others as if they were not there.
  • Appearance: help people look the way they want — clean clothes, hair done, glasses on, dentures in — because how we look affects how we feel.
  • Mealtimes: unhurried, at the table where possible, with the right support and never force-feeding or standing over someone.
  • Continence and personal care: handled discreetly, calmly and without any sign of disgust or impatience.

params.alt: A care worker supporting two older people with an everyday activity at home

params.text:
Don't rush — presence is care

Rushing is one of the most common ways dignity slips away. When people feel hurried, they feel they are a nuisance and stop asking for what they need. Working at the person's pace, giving them time to do what they can for themselves, and staying calm and attentive turns a task into genuine care.

Small courtesies matter: asking permission, saying what you are doing, thanking someone for their patience. These are the everyday habits of the 10 Dignity Do's in action.

params.question: During a busy shift, a colleague calls an 84-year-old man "sweetie" and talks over him to another carer about his incontinence while he sits in the lounge. Which response best upholds his dignity?

answers.text: Address him by the name he prefers, speak directly to him, and discuss any personal care needs privately, not in front of others.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct. Using his chosen name, speaking to him directly and keeping personal matters private all protect his dignity and self-esteem.

answers.text: Say nothing — the colleague is friendly and clearly means no harm.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Good intentions don't make it acceptable. Belittling names and public discussion of personal care undermine dignity and should be challenged.

answers.text: Join in with the nickname so he feels part of a warm, informal team.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Pet names like this can feel patronising to an adult. Unless he has asked to be called this, use the name he prefers.

answers.text: Finish the conversation quickly and move on to the next task.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Rushing past the problem leaves his dignity unprotected. The public discussion of his continence needs is the real issue to address.
